lessons.title: Types of Modules
lessons.summary: Learn the differences between named modules, automatic modules, and unnamed modules, as well as the relationship between the module path and the classpath.

contents.title: Three Types of Modules
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The differences between named [[modules]], automatic modules, and unnamed modules
- The relationship between the [[class]]path and the module path
- Access and export rules based on module type

This is an important topic for understanding module interoperability.

contents.title: Named Modules
contents.body:
Named modules explicitly declare module dependencies and exports in module-info.java.
**exports** specifies packages to make public, and **requires** declares module dependencies.
They must be placed on the module path.
Named modules can explicitly control access from other modules.
This enables powerful [[encapsulation]].

contents.title: Automatic Modules and Unnamed Modules
contents.body:
An automatic module's name is determined from the JAR file's manifest or its file name.
Automatic modules export all packages and require all modules.
The unnamed module contains all classes on the classpath.

contents.title: Difference Between Module Path and Classpath
contents.body:
The module path (--module-path or -p) is the path managed by the module system.
The classpath (-cp or -classpath) is the path used by traditional Java.
JAR files on the module path are treated as automatic modules.
Classes on the classpath belong to the unnamed module.
A named module cannot require the unnamed module.
However, an automatic module can access the unnamed module.

contents.title: Key Points for the Exam
contents.body:
The exam tests the characteristics and constraints of the three module types.
The constraint that "a named module cannot require the unnamed module" is frequently tested.
The fact that an automatic module can serve as a bridge between named modules and the unnamed module is also important.
The jdeps command can be used to analyze module dependencies.

quizzes.question: How is a JAR file without module-info.java treated when placed on the module path?
quizzes.explanation: A JAR file without module-info.java placed on the module path is treated as an automatic module. If placed on the classpath, it belongs to the unnamed module.

quizzes.question: Arrange the steps of the bottom-up approach for migrating classpath-based libraries to the module system in the correct order.
quizzes.explanation: In a bottom-up migration: (1) Identify the lowest-level library in the dependency graph → (2) Add module-info.java to that JAR to make it a named module → (3) Move dependent JARs to the module path (they function as automatic modules) → (4) Sequentially add module-info to higher-level modules to complete the migration.

choices.text: Identify the lowest-level library in the dependency graph
Add module-info.java to that JAR to make it a named module
Move dependent JARs to the module path (automatic module)
Sequentially add module-info to higher-level modules to complete

quizzes.question:
Fill in the short form of the command-line option for specifying the module path.
java _____ mods -m com.example.app/com.example.Main
quizzes.explanation: The short form of --module-path is -p. It is used to specify the module path when running modules.

lessons.title: Service Providers
lessons.summary: Learn about the service provider mechanism in the module system (provides/uses/ServiceLoader).

contents.title: What Are Service Providers?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Providing services with provides ... with
- Declaring service usage with uses
- Dynamically retrieving implementations with **ServiceLoader**

This is the service provider mechanism that achieves loose coupling between [[modules]].

contents.title: The provides...with and uses Directives
contents.body:
The provides directive declares that a module offers an implementation of a service [[interface]].
The syntax is "provides ServiceInterface with ImplementationClass".
The uses directive declares that a module consumes a service.
The syntax is "uses ServiceInterface".
The service provider and consumer can be separated into different modules.

contents.title: Service Provider Implementation Example
contents.body:
The implementation is divided into three parts: the service interface module, the provider module, and the consumer module.
Use [[ServiceLoader]] to dynamically retrieve service implementations.

contents.title: How ServiceLoader Works
contents.body:
**ServiceLoader**.load() lazily loads implementations of a service interface.
If multiple providers exist, all providers are returned in sequence.
You can also use findFirst() to retrieve only the first provider.
Provider classes must have a no-argument public constructor or a static provider() [[method]].
The stream() method allows processing providers as a stream.

contents.title: Service Provider Exam Tips
contents.body:
The implementation class in provides...with does not need to be exported.
If a module uses ServiceLoader without a uses declaration, no services will be found.
The implementation class in provides must implement or [[extend]] the service interface.
A single provides can specify multiple implementation classes separated by commas.
contents.note: The fact that the provides...with implementation class does not need to be **exported** is frequently tested on the exam.

quizzes.question: Find the cause of the problem where ServiceLoader returns no services in the following module-info.java.
quizzes.explanation: To search for services with ServiceLoader, the consumer module must declare `uses com.example.api.Logger;`. Without the uses declaration, ServiceLoader cannot discover service providers.

choices.text: Missing uses directive (uses com.example.api.Logger is needed)
No error (the provider is simply not registered)

quizzes.question:
Complete the service provider declaration.
_____ com.example.api.Logger with com.example.impl.FileLogger;
quizzes.explanation: The provides directive uses the syntax "provides ServiceInterface with ImplementationClass" to declare that the module offers a service implementation.

quizzes.question: Which statement about the implementation class specified in provides...with is correct?
quizzes.explanation: The implementation class specified in provides...with does not need to be exported from the module. The module system's service provider mechanism accesses it internally.

choices.text: It does not need to be exported with exports
It must be exported with exports
It can be an abstract class

quizzes.question: Complete the module package access configuration.
quizzes.explanation: exports allows compile-time access, while opens allows reflective access. Use to for qualified access to specific modules.

lessons.title: opens and Reflection
lessons.summary: Learn about controlling reflective access with the opens directive and deep reflection.

contents.title: Reflection and the Module System
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Allowing [[reflection]] access with the opens directive
- The difference between **exports** and opens
- Why frameworks (Spring, JPA, etc.) require opens

This covers the practically important relationship between reflection and [[modules]].

contents.title: Difference Between exports and opens
contents.body:
exports allows public access at both [[compile]] time and runtime.
**opens** allows reflective access at runtime (including private members).
Even in exported packages, **reflection** cannot access private members.
In opened packages, setAccessible(true) enables access to private members as well.
exports and opens can be used together for the same package.

contents.title: How to Use opens
contents.body:
opens can open a package to all modules or to specific modules only.
Use opens...to to limit the target modules.
You can also declare the entire module as open using open module.

contents.title: Deep Reflection and Normal Access
contents.body:
Normal reflection can only access public members.
Deep Reflection uses setAccessible(true) to access private members.
With exports only, normal reflection is possible but deep reflection is not.
With opens, deep reflection becomes possible.
Performing deep reflection without opens throws an IllegalAccessException.

contents.title: Considerations for open module
contents.body:
In an open module, all packages are implicitly opened.
Even in an open module, exports must be declared separately.
Declaring individual opens within an open module causes a compilation error.
Open modules are convenient for framework-dependent applications, but [[encapsulation]] becomes weaker.
contents.note: In an open module, individual opens cannot be written, but exports can.

quizzes.question: Which statement about the difference between exports and opens is correct?
quizzes.explanation: exports allows compile-time and runtime access to public members, but does not allow reflective access to private members. Using opens enables access to private members via setAccessible(true).

choices.text: opens allows reflective access to private members, but exports does not
exports allows all access including reflective access
opens and exports have exactly the same effect
opens allows compile-time access, but exports allows only runtime access

quizzes.question: Which statement about open modules is correct?
quizzes.explanation: In an open module, all packages are implicitly opened, but exports must still be declared separately for compile-time public access. Declaring individual opens within an open module causes a compilation error.

choices.text: Individual opens declarations within an open module cause a compilation error
In an open module, exports are also performed automatically
Additional opens declarations can be added within an open module
open module was deprecated in Java 17

quizzes.question: The following module-info.java has an error. What is the cause?
quizzes.explanation: In an open module, all packages are implicitly opened, so writing individual opens declarations causes a compilation error. However, exports can still be declared.

choices.text: opens declarations are not allowed within an open module
exports declarations are not allowed within an open module
The position of the open keyword is incorrect
Dots cannot be used in module names

lessons.title: Module Migration
lessons.summary: Learn the migration strategies (bottom-up and top-down) for transitioning from the classpath to the module system.

contents.title: Why Module Migration Is Needed
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Steps to modularize existing [[class]]path-based projects into [[modules]]
- Bottom-up and top-down approaches
- Incremental migration using automatic modules

Learn strategies for modularizing legacy code.

contents.title: Bottom-Up Migration
contents.body:
Bottom-up migration starts by modularizing the lowest-level libraries in the dependency graph.
First, add module-info.java to libraries that have no dependencies on other modules.
Then, sequentially modularize the modules that depend on them.
This approach is recommended because it minimizes dependencies on automatic modules.
However, it can be difficult to apply when the lower-level libraries are not under your control.

contents.title: Top-Down Migration
contents.body:
Top-down migration starts by modularizing the application itself (the top level).
Lower-level libraries are placed on the module path and treated as automatic modules.
Automatic modules can be referenced via requires from named modules.
This approach is effective when you cannot modify the lower-level libraries.
However, it tends to create many dependencies on automatic modules, reducing stability.

contents.title: Automatic Module Naming
contents.body:
The name of an automatic module is determined in two ways.
If the JAR's MANIFEST.MF contains an Automatic-Module-Name attribute, that value is used.
Otherwise, the name is automatically derived from the JAR file name.

contents.title: Migration Considerations
contents.body:
Split packages (the same package existing in multiple modules) are prohibited by the module system.
Split packages that worked without issues on the classpath can cause errors during module migration.
Use the jdeps command to analyze dependencies before planning the migration.
You can also temporarily add access with the --add-reads and --add-exports options.
contents.note: Use jdeps -s to display a dependency summary.

quizzes.question: In a bottom-up migration, which component is modularized first?
quizzes.explanation: In a bottom-up migration, you start by adding module-info.java to the lowest-level libraries that have no dependencies on other modules.

choices.text: The lowest-level library with no dependencies on other libraries
The library with the most classes
Any library can be started with

quizzes.question: Which is the correct way to explicitly specify the name of an automatic module?
quizzes.explanation: You can explicitly specify the name of an automatic module by setting the Automatic-Module-Name attribute in the JAR file's META-INF/MANIFEST.MF. This is the recommended approach.

choices.text: Set the Automatic-Module-Name attribute in MANIFEST.MF
Specify it with a command-line option

quizzes.question: In the module system, having the same package exist in multiple modules — known as a "_____ package" — is prohibited.
quizzes.explanation: A split package occurs when the same package spans multiple modules. While this was tolerated on the classpath, it causes an error in the module system.
